summary: Game-cheat package with disguised persistence, hidden supervisor process, and obfuscated remote-licensed payload
details: On require() or CLI invocation, launcher.js spawns a detached, stdio-ignored Node process (windowsHide:true) that renames itself to 'Runtime Broker' (impersonating the Windows system process), then exits the original parent so nothing visible happens. The supervisor writes an HKCU Run-key entry named 'OneDriveUpdate' that points wscript.exe at a shipped 'OneDrive.Standalone.Updater.vbs' which re-launches the package — persistence disguised as a Microsoft OneDrive auto-updater rather than the package's own name. launcher.js then reads server.obf.js (heavily obfuscator.io-encoded, RC4 string array, ~81KB) and passes it directly to eval(); the decoded module forks another supervisor, builds a hardware fingerprint from the HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Cryptography MachineGuid + os.hostname() + C: volume serial, SHA-256s it, and POSTs hwid+key+hostname to a license endpoint whose hostname is hidden inside the RC4-decoded string array. console.log/warn/error are monkey-patched to rewrite 'sens.node' to 'program module', replace the install path with '<app>', and silently drop any line containing 'program module loaded' — active concealment of the package's own output from anyone running it interactively. The shipped 6.8MB sens.node Windows PE contains symbol strings ('Teleport','LeftFoot','LeftHand','Head') and the package's own UI (public/index.html) advertises Aimbot, Triggerbot, ESP, and Vehicle Spawn — i.e. an FPS game-cheat / memory-injection tool distributed via npm with malware-grade evasion (process-name impersonation, Microsoft-impersonating persistence, log scrubbing, eval-of-obfuscated-blob, hidden-host beacon).

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
